coredata.dc:title: Preparation of amino-modified carbon quantum dots-ZnO/cellulose nanofiber multifunctional hydrogel: Enhanced adsorption synergistic photoreduction and reversible fluorescence response visual recognition of Cr(VI)
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Qing
You, Yong
Hu, Xingyu
Lu, Danqing
Wen, Qian
Yu, Gang
Wang, Wenlei
Xu, Tao
coredata.dc:description: This work innovatively used cellulose nanofibers as a photocatalyst carrier, which could recycle nano-photocatalysts and minimize nanoparticle aggregation. The morphology, structures, chemical composition, optical-electronic properties and photocatalytic performance of amino-modified carbon quantum dots-ZnO/cellulose nanofiber (N-CQDs-ZnO/CNF: ZCH-2) hydrogel were characterized by SEM, TEM, BET, EDS, XRD, FTIR, UV–vis, XPS, PL and other techniques. The mechanism of Cr(VI) adsorption synergistic photoreduction by ZCH-2 was discussed in detail. The results showed that the prepared ZCH-2 had excellent removal performance for Cr(VI). After 120 min of adsorption and 40 min of photoreduction, the removal efficiency of Cr(VI) was 98.9 %. Compared with ZnO/CNF hydrogel, the adsorption performance of ZCH-2 increased by 268 % and the photoreduction performance increased by 116 %. The adsorption of Cr(VI) by ZCH-2 was controlled by electrostatic attraction and chemical adsorption. The photoreduction kinetic constant of ZCH-2 was 0.106 min−1, which was 8.9 times that of ZnO/CNF hydrogel. The N-CQDs in ZCH-2 could form N-CQDs-metal complexes with Cr(VI), resulting in fluorescence quenching, so Cr(VI) could be visually identified by fluorescence changes. This study provides a new idea for the design and optimization of a new multifunctional hydrogel with efficient adsorption-photoreduction-fluorescence recognition.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amino-modified carbon quantum dots
Cellulose nanofiber hydrogel
Cr(VI) adsorption synergistic photoreduction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
Preparation of N-CQDs-ZnO cellulose nanofiber fluorescent hydrogel
The removal and reversible sensing mechanism

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Scientific Research Foundation of Education Office of Hunan Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is jointly funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51974115 ) and the Scientific Research Foundation of Education Office of Hunan Province (No. 22C0111 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of porous N-CQDs-ZnO/CNF fluorescent hydrogel.
FTIR (a), XPS full spectrum (b), XPS of Zn2p (c), XPS of C1s (d), XPS of O1s (e), EDS of ZCH-2 (f), SEM of ZCH-2 (g, h).
XRD of ZnO, ZCH-0 and ZCH-2 (a), TEM of ZCH-2 (b), TEM of N-CQDs in ZCH-2 (c), Mapping of ZCH-2 (d, e, f, g, h).
The c/c 0 diagram (a,b,c), Pseudo-first-order adsorption kinetics and pseudo-second-order adsorption kinetics (d,e,f) diagram, Pseudo-first-order photoreduction kinetic diagram (g,h,i).
The cycle performance of ZCH-2 (a), removal efficiency of Cr(VI) (b), UV–vis DRS diagram (c), Eg values (d), valence band (e), PL (f), band gap (g, h).
Full spectrum (a), O1s spectrum (b), Zn2p spectrum (c), and N1s spectrum (d), of ZCH-2.

Physical images of ZCH-0 and ZCH-2 under UV lamp irradiation (a), UV–vis, PL and PLE of ZCH-2 (b), PL spectra of ZCH-2 under different excitations (c), PL emission spectra of ZCH-2 after dark adsorption synergistic photoreduction (d), original physical image of ZCH-2 under UV lamp irradiation (left), after dark adsorption (middle) and after photoreduction (right) (e), Cr2p after dark adsorption synergistic photoreduction of ZCH-2 (f), Adsorption synergistic photoreduction mechanism (g).

head.ce:title: Preparation of amino-modified carbon quantum dots-ZnO/cellulose nanofiber multifunctional hydrogel: Enhanced adsorption synergistic photoreduction and reversible fluorescence response visual recognition of Cr(VI)

Central South University of Forestry and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work innovatively used cellulose nanofibers as a photocatalyst carrier, which could recycle nano-photocatalysts and minimize nanoparticle aggregation. The morphology, structures, chemical composition, optical-electronic properties and photocatalytic performance of amino-modified carbon quantum dots-ZnO/cellulose nanofiber (N-CQDs-ZnO/CNF: ZCH-2) hydrogel were characterized by SEM, TEM, BET, EDS, XRD, FTIR, UV–vis, XPS, PL and other techniques. The mechanism of Cr(VI) adsorption synergistic photoreduction by ZCH-2 was discussed in detail. The results showed that the prepared ZCH-2 had excellent removal performance for Cr(VI). After 120 min of adsorption and 40 min of photoreduction, the removal efficiency of Cr(VI) was 98.9 %. Compared with ZnO/CNF hydrogel, the adsorption performance of ZCH-2 increased by 268 % and the photoreduction performance increased by 116 %. The adsorption of Cr(VI) by ZCH-2 was controlled by electrostatic attraction and chemical adsorption. The photoreduction kinetic constant of ZCH-2 was 0.106 min−1, which was 8.9 times that of ZnO/CNF hydrogel. The N-CQDs in ZCH-2 could form N-CQDs-metal complexes with Cr(VI), resulting in fluorescence quenching, so Cr(VI) could be visually identified by fluorescence changes. This study provides a new idea for the design and optimization of a new multifunctional hydrogel with efficient adsorption-photoreduction-fluorescence recognition.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The urgency of environmental degradation and energy scarcity has escalated in tandem with rapid advancements in science and technology. Sectors such as electroplating, printing, dyeing, painting, battery manufacturing, metal processing, and tanning have generated a significant amount of wastewater, frequently containing chromium (Cr) [1]. Excessive intake of Cr could cause lung and liver damage, and even induce cancer [2]. Cr(VI) is more toxic than Cr(III), and usually exists in the forms of CrO3, CrO4 2− and Cr2O7 2− [3]. Therefore, the creation of an eco-friendly, cost-effective, and efficient remediation technology for Cr(VI) pollution is crucial for the preservation of the ecological environment and the sustainable utilization of water resources [4].

The methods commonly used to remove Cr(VI) from wastewater included chemical methods [5], electrochemistry [6], adsorption [7], photocatalysis [8] and biological methods [9]. Adsorption is one of these techniques that is often utilized due to its ease of use and effectiveness [10]. However, its disadvantage is that it cannot convert highly toxic Cr(VI) into less toxic Cr(III), which is easy to cause secondary pollution [11]. In order to solve these problems, photocatalytic technology is considered as one of the most effective methods, because it can convert highly toxic Cr(VI) into less harmful Cr(III) [12]. ZnO is an n-type semiconductor with a band gap of 3.37 eV. ZnO semiconductor photocatalyst has been widely studied due to its non-toxicity, good biocompatibility and low price [13]. Using chitosan(CS)-supported zirconium (Zr)-ZnO, Preethi et al. [14] created a photocatalyst for Cr(VI) reduction. Zr-ZnO@CS improved Cr(VI) removal by band gap localization, charge excitation, and increased dynamic sites. However, the wide band gap of ZnO only utilizes 5 % of the photon energy, and the electron-hole pairs on its surface are prone to recombination (low quantum efficiency), which restricts its broad application [15]. The tunable adjustment of the band structure, achieved by integrating semiconductor photocatalytic materials such as N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), can enhance the photocatalyst's utilization of low-energy light excitation. This feature significantly amplifies its potential applications in the field of photocatalysis [16].

N-CQDs are a new type of fluorescent carbon-based materials, which have the advantages of diverse sources, low cost, low toxicity, rich surface functional groups and good biocompatibility [17]. There are a large number of O/N functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs, such as -COOH, -OH and -NH2, which is beneficial to the adsorption of Cr(VI). Due to the π-conjugated structure, N-CQDs have charge receptor and transport function, which is conducive to the conduction of photogenerated carriers. Therefore, N-CQDs have a wide range of applications in the field of photoreduction. The formation of nanoscale heterojunctions between N-CQDs and ZnO prolongs the duration and channel of charge transfer, improves the photoresponsivity and e−/h+ separation of ZnO, maximizes the use of light energy, and further improves the photocatalytic activity of ZnO. In summary, N-CQDs can be used as a multifunctional component in the composite photocatalyst to further improve the removal ability of Cr(VI) by adsorption synergistic photoreduction. In addition, as a promising fluorescent material, N-CQDs have the ability of fluorescence quenching or enhancement. It can be used to detect and identify Cr(VI) with the naked eye, which is more convenient and economical than traditional detection methods. Huang et al. [18] innovatively incorporated N-CQDs into a polyacrylamide hydrogel network to prepare a ‘smart switch sensor’ hydrogel, which could visually identify Cu2+ and Ag+ through reversible fluorescence response. In this study, N-CQDs-ZnO composite photocatalyst was constructed using suitable way to optimize adsorption synergistic photoreduction performance.

Although the synthesis methods of N-CQDs-ZnO composite nanomaterials have been reported in the literature, these studies mainly focus on the use of nanoparticles as the research object. There are still two urgent problems to be solved. First, smaller N-CQDs-ZnO nanoparticles have higher surface energy and are prone to agglomeration. Second, nanoparticles are difficult to recycle and reuse [19]. In order to overcome these problems, N-CQDs-ZnO can be dispersed in the elastic network structure of hydrogels. Cellulose nanofiber (CNF) can be used as the ‘skeleton’ of hydrogel with three-dimension (3D) porous structure, which is an ideal photocatalyst carrier [20]. Yuan et al. [21] used cellulose nanofibers and carbon dots to synthesize a novel fluorescent lignin-based hydrogel for the evaluation of Cr(VI) adsorption performance. The maximum adsorption capacity of the hydrogel for Cr(VI) was 599.9 mg/g. CNF not only have the advantages of easy degradation, renewable, non-toxic and low cost, but also can promote the construction of pores. These pores have a large specific surface area and increase more catalytic reaction sites. In addition, small-sized cellulose can expose more hydroxyl groups on the surface of cellulose to form stronger hydrogen bonds. The high hydrogen bond strength makes the binding of CNF with N-CQDs and ZnO stronger [22].

In this study, we innovatively prepared a multifunctional hydrogel (N-CQDs-ZnO/CNF) by combining N-CQDs and nano-ZnO into the cellulose nanofiber hydrogel network for the removal of Cr(VI). Various techniques were used to examine the morphology, chemical composition, functional group, crystal facet parameter, optical-electronic properties and photocatalytic performance of the multifunctional hydrogels. The effects of Cr(VI) removal under different N-CQDs content, pH and initial Cr(VI) concentration were systematically studied. The adsorption and photoreduction behavior of Cr(VI) were also discussed. The reversible fluorescence sensing visual recognition mechanism of the multifunctional hydrogel for Cr(VI) was explored. Finally, a new mechanism of N-CQDs enhancing the synergistic effect of ZnO-CNF on Cr(VI) adsorption, photoreduction and sensing were proposed.

Acrylic acid (AA), N,N-acetyl-dipropylamine (MBA), potassium sulfate (KPS), and potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) were all purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. Urea (CH4N2O), citric acid (C6H8O7) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Guoyao Group Chemical Co., Ltd. All the above chemicals were analytically pure and could be used without further purification. Carboxylated cellulose nanofibers were purchased from Huzhou ScienceK Ltd. Macklin Co., Ltd. supplied nano ZnO dispersion (50 wt% in H2O). Ultrapure water equipment (ZC1-A10Y, Changsha Zhichuang, China) prepared all the deionized (DI) water for the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Amino-modified carbon quantum dots were prepared by hydrothermal reaction. The specific preparation method were as follows: (1) Urea (3 g), citric acid (6 g) and DI water (30 mL) were added to a 250 mL beaker in turn, and ultrasonicated for 6 min to obtain a settled solution. (2) The settled solution was poured into a high-pressure reactor lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and placed in a muffle furnace. The solvent heat treatment was carried out at 160 °C for 4 h. (3) After the hydrothermal reaction was completed, the synthesized product was cooled to room temperature, and the obtained solution was washed with DI water and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. In order to further remove the unreacted impurities, the filtrate was placed in a dialysis bag for 36 h, and DI water was replaced every 6 h. Finally, the purified N-CQDs were obtained after freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-CQDs-ZnO cellulose nanofiber fluorescent hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: A 3D porous N-CQDs-ZnO cellulose nanofiber (N-CQDs-ZnO/CNF) fluorescent hydrogel was prepared by free radical polymerization. The specific methods were as follows: (1) CNF and acrylic acid with a mass ratio of 1:1 were added to the beaker and mixed evenly. (2) Added 3.5 mL NaOH (5 mol/L) solution and dissolved in ice water bath. (3) The ZnO dispersion (wt% = 10 %) was added to the product prepared in step (1), and ultrasonicated for 15 min to make the ZnO more uniformly dispersed in the mixed solution. (4) When N-CQDs was uniformly dispersed in the beaker solution, different volumes of N-CQDs (0 μL, 100 μL, 150 μL, 200 μL) were added to the solution of step (3) and sonicated for 2 min. (5) 0.016 g N, N-methylenebisacrylamide (crosslinking agent) was added to the beaker and ultrasonically dissolved. (6) After adding 0.015 g potassium persulfate (initiator), the O2 in the reaction system was removed by N2 purging. (7) The mixed solution was poured into a small glass tube and heated at 60 °C for 2 h. Finally, four groups of hydrogels were obtained, which were recorded as ZCH-0 (0 μL) (without N-CQDs), ZCH-1 (100 μL), ZCH-2 (150 μL) and ZCH-3 (200 μL), respectively. The prepared hydrogel was immersed in DI water for 36 h, and the DI water was replaced every 12 h. The synthesis process of N-CQDs-ZnO/CNF hydrogel was shown in Fig. 1 .

Dark adsorption studies were carried out using different components of hydrogels and different experimental conditions. The sample (0.1 g dry weight) was soaked in DI water for 2 h before to use, and then it was placed into a 250 mL conical flask containing a Cr(VI) solution (200 mL). NaOH or H2SO4 solution (1 mol/L) was added to the mixture to bring the pH level up or down to the desired range. Equipartitioned samples of the solution (5 mL) were taken at predetermined intervals and filtered through a syringe filter (d = 0.22 μm). The concentration of Cr(VI) was determined using the 1,5-diphenylcarbazide method with a UV–visible spectrophotometer. In order to ensure the reliability of the experimental data, all experiments were carried out three times. The error bars in the figure represent the standard deviation from the mean value. Removal efficiency and adsorption capacity (q e) are calculated as follows:
(1)
Removal efficiency
%
=
c
0 −
c e
c 0
× 100 %
(2)
q e
=
c 0
V 0
−
c e
V e
m
where q e (mg/g) is the equilibrium adsorption capacity. c 0 and c e (mg/L) are the initial concentration and final concentration of Cr(VI), respectively. m (g) is the mass of dry hydrogel. V 0 and V e (L) are the initial volume and equilibrium volume at adsorption process, respectively.

Adsorption kinetics (reaction order and rate constant) were used to assess the efficacy of the N-CQDs-ZnO/CNF hydrogel adsorbent for Cr(VI). Adsorption may be defined in terms of its time-dependent kinetic properties using either pseudo-first-order or pseudo-second-order kinetics [23]. The most often used adsorption kinetic models and formulae are as follows:
(3)
log
q e
−
q t
= log
q e
−
k 1
t
2.303
(4)
t
q t
=
1
k 2
q e 2
+
t
q e
where t (min) is adsorption time. q
t and q
e (mg/g) are the adsorption capacity at t min and at adsorption equilibrium, respectively. k 1 (min−1) and k 2 (g·mg−1·min−1) are the pesudo-first-order and pesudo-second-order kinetic rate constant, respectively.

After dark adsorption for 120 min, N-CQDs-ZnO/CNF hydrogel and the solution containing Cr(VI) were poured into a 250 mL photoreactor and irradiated under a 500 W mercury lamp (≤ 420 nm). The concentration of Cr(VI) in the solution was measured at 10, 20, 30, and 40 min of exposure time. In order to accommodate Cr(VI)’s photoreduction properties, the pseudo-first-order reaction kinetic model was applied. The calculation formula is as follows:
(5)
ln
c 0
/ c
=
k 3
t
where c
o and c (mg/L) are the initial and t min concentration of Cr(VI), respectively. k 3 (min−1) was the rate constant of photoreduction.
The characterizations were displayed in the Text. S1 of the supplementary material.

The FTIR spectra of ZnO, N-CQDs, ZCH-0 and ZCH-2 were shown in Fig. 2a. The stretching vibration maxima of -OH and NH were found to be 3440 cm−1 and 3200 cm−1, respectively. The frequency of the CH stretching vibration was 2805 cm−1. The frequency of CO stretching vibration was 1550 cm−1. This indicated that after adding acrylic acid, the carboxylated CNF has a large amount of -OH and -COOH. The carboxylated CNF has high electronegativity, which was helpful for electrostatic adsorption of positively charged particles (such as Zn2+). After adding NaOH solution, Zn2+ reacted with OH− to form Zn(OH)4 2− and grow into a ZnO crystal nucleus, so ZnO was uniformly dispersed in CNF [24]. And ZnO was very stable in the hydrogel due to the strong interaction between Zn2+ and -COOH.

The frequency of CN stretching vibrations was 1162 cm−1 [25]. It could be inferred that the surface of N-CQDs has abundant active groups such as CO, -OH and -NH2 groups. Due to the hydrophilicity of N-CQDs, the strong binding of N-CQDs to hydrogels became easier [26]. ZCH-0 and ZCH-2 showed the same peak type as ZnO in the range of 950–850 cm−1, indicating that ZnO had been successfully connected to hydrogel [27]. The NH stretching vibration peak at 3200 cm−1 was not obvious in ZCH-2, which may be due to the fact that N-CQDs were encapsulated into the 3D porous structure of CNF hydrogel.

The XPS full spectrum in Fig. 2b shown that the ZCH-2 contains Zn, C, N, O elements. Fig. 2c showed the Zn2p peak of ZCH-2, and the binding energies were 1021.98 eV (Zn2p3/2) and 1045.04 eV (Zn2p1/2), respectively [28]. The binding energy difference between Zn2p1/2 and Zn2p3/2 was 23.06 eV, which was consistent with the standard value of ZnO [29]. As shown in the Cls spectrum of Fig. 2d, it was shown that there were CC bonds, Zn-O-C bonds and CO bonds in ZCH-0 and ZCH-2 [30]. In Fig. 2e, The increased in the binding energy of 531.26 eV (Zn-OH bond) and 533.22 eV (CO bond) [31] of ZCH-2 was due to the change in electron cloud density with the C atoms surrounding the N and O atoms [32]. This confirmed that the -NH2 group in N-CQDs was successfully connected to the CNF hydrogel. The EDS spectrum (Fig. 2f) shown that the hydrogel mainly contained C, O, N, Na and Zn elements. This was consistent with the conclusion of the XPS full spectrum. Fig. 2g and h show the porous structure of ZCH-2 under different scales, and fine particles were observed on the pore surface of the hydrogel. The porous structure of ZCH-2 was conducived to the dispersion of N-CQDs and ZnO in the CNF hydrogel, and promoted the adsorption synergistic photoreduction of Cr(VI). In addition, the BET specific surface areas of ZCH-0 and ZCH-2 were 0.628 m2/g and 1.406 m2/g, respectively. It shows that ZCH-2 has a higher specific surface area than ZCH-0. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms also confirmed the 3D porous structure of ZCH-2 (Supplementary Material Figs. S1-S2).

The lattice spacing of N-CQDs and ZnO were calculated by Bragg equation (d = nλ/2sinθ), where d is the lattice spacing and n is a positive integer. It can be seen from Fig. 3a that the diffraction peaks at 31.769°, 34.421°, 36.252°, 56.602° and 62.862° correspond to the (100), (002), (101), (110), and (103) crystal planes of ZnO (PDF#36–1451), respectively. The diffraction peaks of ZCH-0 and ZCH-2 did not change significantly, which may be due to the encapsulation of N-CQDs and ZnO by amorphous hydrogels, and similar results were reported in related studies [33].

Fine particles observed on the surface of the pores of hydrogels were further characterized by TEM. The heterojunction between ZnO and N-CQDs could be clearly observed in Fig. 3b. The lattice spacing of ZnO was 0.26 nm, corresponding to the (101) crystal plane of ZnO (PDF#36–1451). This result was consistent with the results of XRD analysis. The lattice spacing of N-CQDs was 0.22 nm, corresponding to the interplanar spacing of graphite carbon (002) [34]. Fig. 3c showed the TEM of N-CQDs in ZCH-2, and the size of N-CQDs were about 4–7 nm. The mapping shown in Fig. 3d-h provided confirmation that the elements C, O, N, and Zn were distributed in an even manner throughout ZCH-2.

As shown in Fig. 4a, the removal efficiency of Cr(VI) by ZCH-0, ZCH-1, ZCH-2 and ZCH-3 at 120 min of dark adsorption were 5 %, 13.8 %, 18.4 % and 12.1 %, respectively. After photoreduction (40 min), the removal efficiency of Cr(VI) were 42.3 %, 87.4 %, 98.9 % and 59.2 %, respectively. The results showed that the adsorption synergistic photoreduction performance of ZCH-0 (without N-CQDs) was not ideal. Compared with ZCH-0, the adsorption performance of ZCH-2 increased by 268 % and the photoreduction performance increased by 116 %. This indicated that the addition of an appropriate amount of N-CQDs could enhance the adsorption capacity and photoreduction efficiency of ZnO/CNF hydrogels for Cr(VI). The hydrogel formed by an appropriate amount of N-CQDs and ZnO/CNF contains abundant -OH, -COOH, -NH2, which is very conducive to the adsorption of Cr(VI) by the hydrogel. When the amount of N-CQDs exceeded 150 μL, the photoreduction performance of the N-CQDs-ZnO/CNF hydrogel was significantly reduced, which may be due to the excessive adhesion of N-CQDs on the surface of nano-ZnO [16]. When an appropriate amount of N-CQDs was added, the heterojunction formed on nano-ZnO and N-CQDs were excited by light to generate electron and hole pairs, which was conducive to generating more carriers [17]. Therefore, when the content of N-CQDs was 150 μL, the hydrogel has the best removal performance for Cr(VI).

As shown in Fig. 4b, acidic conditions have a significant effect on the adsorption synergistic photoreduction performance of hydrogels. When the pH was 2, 5, 8 and 11, and the dark adsorption time was 120 min, the removal efficiencies of Cr(VI) by ZCH-2 were 18.4 %, 16.9 %, 9.5 % and 7.3 %, respectively. After photoreduction for 40 min, the removal efficiencies of Cr(VI) by ZCH-2 were 98.9 %, 91.6 %, 49.3 % and 33.4 %, respectively. Under acidic conditions, the main forms of Cr(VI) solution were HCrO4 − and Cr2O7 2−, which was conducive to the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) (HCrO4 − + 3e− + 7H+ → 4H2O + Cr3+, Cr2O7 2− + 6e− + 14H+ → 7H2O + 2Cr3+) [35]. In alkaline conditions, the main form of Cr(VI) in solution was CrO4 2−. The species distribution of Cr(VI) at different pH were shown in Fig. S3 [23]. The Zeta potential of ZCH-0 and ZCH-2 at different pH were shown in Fig. S4. With the increase of pH, the more negative the potential of ZCH-2, the more unfavorable the adsorption of CrO4 2−. Therefore, when the pH was 2, the removal efficiency of Cr(VI) by ZCH-2 was the highest.

Fig. 4c showed the effect of initial concentration on the removal of Cr(VI) at pH = 2. When the initial concentration were 15 mg/L, 20 mg/L, 25 mg/L and 30 mg/L, the removal efficiency of Cr(VI) by ZCH-2 were 100 %, 98.9 %, 91.7 % and 64.0 %, respectively (the adsorption synergistic photoreduction time was 160 min). The adsorption capacity of Cr(VI) by ZCH-2 increased with the increase of Cr(VI) concentration, but the removal efficiency decreased. This was because when most of the adsorption sites were occupied by Cr(VI), the adsorption sites on ZCH-2 were limited, so the adsorption efficiency was reduced.

The hydrogel was first stirred in the dark for 120 min to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium, and then irradiated by ultraviolet light for 40 min. The kinetic mechanism of Cr(VI) adsorption were analyzed by pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models, and the fitting data were shown in Tables S1, S2, S3 (k 1, k 2, k 3 and R 2). As shown in Fig. 4d, e, f, the results showed that the adsorption capacity of ZCH-2 for Cr(VI) was 13 mg/g (initial concentration of Cr(VI) = 20 mg/L, pH = 2, t = 120 min), and the correlation coefficient of the pseudo-second-order model (R 2 = 0.981) was slightly higher than that of the pseudo-first-order model (R 2 = 0.977). Therefore, the adsorption of Cr(VI) by ZCH-2 hydrogel was mainly dominated by electrostatic attraction and chemical adsorption. The photoreduction reaction rate can be well described by the pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics (Fig. 4g, h, i). The photoreduction performance of the prepared hydrogel was evaluated by the reaction rate constant. ZCH-2 had the highest apparent removal rate constant (k 3 = 0.106 min−1), which was 8.9, 2.2 and 5.7 times that of ZCH-0 (0.0119 min−1), ZCH-1 (0.048 min−1) and ZCH-3 (0.0186 min−1), respectively. Compared with ZnO/CNF hydrogel without N-CQDs, the removal efficiency of Cr(VI) by N-CQDs-ZnO/CNF hydrogel was significantly improved. This was because the loading of N-CQDs not only enhanced the adsorption performance of amino induced Cr(VI), but also improved the photocatalytic performance of ZnO/CNF hydrogel, thereby improving the removal efficiency of Cr(VI).

The recycling performance of ZCH-2 was shown in Fig. 5a and b. 0.5 g/L ZCH-2 was added to 200 mL Cr(VI) solution (initial Cr(VI) concentration = 20 mg/L, pH = 2). ZCH-2 was used repeatedly for 5 times under the same conditions (adsorption process: 120 min, photoreduction process: 40 min). After each cycle, the ZCH-2 was washed with DI water, dried and reused. The ZCH-2 still showed good photoreduction performance after 5 cycles, and the removal efficiency decreased from 98.9 % to 86.6 %. After one cycle, the concentration of Zn2+ in the solution was measured to be 4.19 mg/L, indicating that a small amount of ZnO was dissolved and released in the solution under acidic conditions. However, due to the strong electrostatic adsorption and hydrogen-bonding interactions between ZnO and the -COOH on the ZCH-2, the loading amount of ZnO on the ZCH-2 was stable. In addition, it showed excellent removal performance for Cr(VI) in cycle experiments, which confirmed that ZCH-2 still had good stability under acidic conditions. We also found that this study has very good experimental results through literature comparison. Table S4 provided a comparative study of adsorption synergistic photoreduction for Cr(VI) removal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The removal and reversible sensing mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The photoreduction mechanism of Cr(VI) by ZCH-2 was analyzed by band structure and PL spectrum. As shown in Fig. 5c, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of ZCH-2 has a wide absorption band at about 273 nm. The strong adsorption peaks below 300 nm were usually attributed to the π-π* transition of the conjugated CC bond and the n-π* transition of the CO bond [36]. As shown in Fig. 5d, the intersection of the high absorption region and the X-axis was the band gap energy value of the sample according to the Kubelka-Munk equation. It can be found that the band gap of ZCH-2 was 2.61 eV, which was less than the band gap energy of ZCH-0 (3.47 eV). It also indicated that the heterostructure of N-CQDs bound to ZnO favored reducing the band gap energy, expanded the optical absorption spectrum and improved the utilization of solar energy. The valence bands of ZCH-0 and ZCH-2 were measured to be 2.97 eV and 2.85 eV by XPS (Fig. 5e). The PL of ZCH-0 and ZCH-2 were shown in Fig. 5f and ZCH-0 has a strong emission peak at 470 nm. With the addition of N-CQDs, the emission peak of ZCH-2 was blue-shifted to 460 nm, and the emission peak intensity was significantly reduced. This was mainly due to the extension of carrier lifetime and the decreased of electron-hole pair recombination rate, indicating that ZCH-2 had higher photocatalytic activity than ZCH-0. This phenomenon was attributed to the high electron transport of the spatially extended π-conjugated system of N-CQDs [37]. Fig. 5g and h showed the band gap of ZCH-0 and ZCH-2. Based on the above results, the possible mechanism for the significant enhancement of photocatalytic performance of ZnO driven by N-CQDs in this system was as follows: the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy level of N-CQDs released electrons to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy level of N-CQDs, and then transferred to the conduction band (CB) of ZnO. The addition of N-CQDs caused a negative shift of the Fermi level of ZnO, resulting in the easier transfer of photogenerated electrons in the CB of ZnO, and the reduction reaction with Cr(VI) at the photocatalyst site to generate Cr(III) [38].

Fig. 6a is the full spectrum of ZCH-2 (Initial sample, after adsorption, after photocatalysis). From the O1s spectrum of ZCH-2 (Fig. 6b), it could be seen that 531.43 eV and 533.23 eV were the characteristic peaks of Zn-O-Zn and Zn-O-H, respectively. After dark adsorption and photoreduction, the binding energy of CO and CO increased due to the change of electron cloud density of C atoms adjacent to N and O atoms, which proved that N-CQDs enhanced electron transfer. As shown in Fig. 6c, Zn2p3/2 (1021.98 eV) and Zn2p1/2 (1045.08 eV) were the characteristic peaks of ZCH-2 [39]. After dark adsorption synergistic photoreduction, both binding energy corresponding to Zn-O-Zn and Zn-OH were shifted to the right, which indicated that the -OH and -COOH on the surface attract Cr(VI) and Cr(III) through static electricity during the photoreduction of Cr(VI).

In photoreduction process, the migration of photogenerated electrons from N-CQDs to ZnO was carried out as an electron acceptor [40]. As can be seen from Fig. 6d, the spectra of N1s after dark adsorption and photoreduction could be divided into two peaks at 399.78 and 401.78 eV, which were attributed to the nitrogen atoms in C-N-C and C-N-H, respectively. This showed that Cr(VI) and reduced Cr(III) mainly reacted with C-N-H, indicating that the amino group attracts Cr(VI) and Cr(III) through static electricity, and also confirming that there were both physical and chemical interactions in the adsorption of Cr(VI) and reduced Cr(III) [41]. In addition, C-N-H peak appeared after dark adsorption synergistic photoreduction, which proved that ZCH-2 has a chelating effect with Cr(VI), and then Cr(VI) was further photoreduced to Cr(III).

Fig. 7a showed that ZCH-0 (left) and ZCH-2 (right) under 365 nm UV light irradiation, ZCH-2 hydrogel showed bright blue fluorescence. Fig. 7b showed the UV–vis, photoluminescence excitation (PL) and emission spectra (PLE) of ZCH-2. The results showed that the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of ZCH-2 were 360 nm and 466 nm, respectively. The PL spectrum of ZCH-2 shown in Fig. 7c also verified that the maximum emission peak was at about 466 nm when excited at 370 nm. With the increase of excitation wavelength from 310 nm to 450 nm, the emission peak of ZCH-2 gradually red-shifted, and the emission wavelength of ZCH-2 was the largest when the excitation wavelength was 370 nm. As the excitation wavelength was altered, there was a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity. This change was due to the aggregation caused by the presence of O-containing functional groups [4]. Fig. 7d was the PL emission spectrum after adsorption and photoreduction of Cr(VI), and the maximum emission wavelength had a blue shift, which may be due to the chelation between ZCH-2 and Cr(VI) [18].

In Fig. 7e, the fluorescence intensity of ZCH-2 decreased significantly after the adsorption equilibrium of Cr(VI). This might be because the surface of N-CQDs were rich in O-containing and N-containing functional groups, which were easy to form coordination complexes with Cr(VI) [3]. The research results of Huang et al. [18] confirmed that the fluorescence quenching of CQDs was attributed to the electron transfer between CQDs and metal ions. When metal ions were in contact with N-CQDs, the O-containing and N-containing functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs could combine with metal ions to form N-CQDs-metal complexes. The electrons present on the surface of N-CQDs can be readily transferred to metal ions, altering the surface state of N-CQDs and consequently resulting in fluorescence quenching. However, due to the red shift of λ MAX after the interaction between ZCH-2 and Cr(VI), it indicated that it experienced a static quenching mechanism [42]. Based on the rapid visual response to Cr(VI), ZCH-2 fluorescent hydrogel showed efficient detection of Cr(VI). Cr(III) has no direct fluorescence quenching effect, so the fluorescence performance of ZCH-2 was restored [43]. Therefore, ZCH-2 can not only reduce Cr(VI) to Cr(III), but also the fluorescence reversibility of ZCH-2 enables us to visually identify Cr(VI). The peaks of Cr2p in Fig. 7f at 586.38 eV and 577.38 eV combined energy were Cr(VI) and Cr(III), respectively [44]. The change of valence states of the elements further shown that the photocarrier reduction of Cr(VI) was reasonable. Due to the presence of Cr(III), this further proved that the removal of Cr(VI) by ZCH-2 was the result of the combined action of adsorption and photoreduction. To elucidate the removal mechanism of Cr, we further quantitatively analyzed the changes in Cr(VI) and its reduction intermediates Cr(III) during adsorption synergistic photoreduction on ZCH-0 and ZCH-2 (Fig. S5). During the adsorption process, trace Cr(III) formation was found in ZCH-0 and ZCH-2 immersion solutions, which can be mainly attributed to the presence of ZnO. In the photoreduction process, the decrease of Cr(VI) concentration and increase of Cr(III) concentration followed the same order, i.e. ZCH-2 > ZCH-0, which indicated that NCH-2 has better photoreduction ability. In summary, N-CQDs have an important role in the reduction of Cr(VI).

Based on the above analysis, the adsorption synergistic photoreduction mechanism and reversible sensing mechanism of ZCH-2 for Cr(VI) may be as follows (Fig. 7g): (1) The functional groups such as -OH, -NH2 and -COOH on the surface of ZCH-2 enhanced the interaction between Cr(VI) and ZCH-2 through electrostatic attraction and chemical adsorption. (2) Under the irradiation of ultraviolet light, the HOMO level of N-CQDs released electrons to the LUMO level, and then transferred to the CB of ZnO. The addition of N-CQDs makes the Fermi level of ZnO shift negatively, which leads to the easier transfer of photogenerated electrons in the CB of ZnO to form e− and h+ (ZCH-2 + hν → ZCH-2 (e−, h+)). In an acidic environment, the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) was facilitated by the acquisition of electrons (Cr2O7 2− + 14H+ + 6e− → 2Cr3+ + 7H2O). (3) N-CQDs formed N-CQDs-metal complexes with Cr(VI), resulting in fluorescence quenching of ZCH-2. Since Cr(III) generated by the photoreduction reaction has no direct quenching effect, ZCH-2 has restored its fluorescence properties. (4) Part of the reduced Cr(III) was released into the solution, and the remaining Cr(III) forms Cr(OH)3.

The cellulose nanofibers could construct porous structure and mass transfer channels and provide more binding sites, thus greatly reducing the agglomeration of N-CQDs-ZnO nanoparticles. Under the conditions of 150 μL of N-CQDs solution, pH = 2 and initial Cr(VI) concentration of 20 mg/L, the maximum removal efficiency of Cr(VI) by ZCH-2 reached 98.9 % after 160 mins of adsorption synergistic photoreduction process. The adsorption of Cr(VI) by ZCH-2 followed the pseudo-second-order kinetic model, indicating that the adsorption process was mainly affected by electrostatic interaction and chemical adsorption. Compared with ZCH-0, the adsorption performance of ZCH-2 increased by 268 % and the photoreduction performance increased by 116 %. The pseudo-first-order photoreduction kinetic rate constant of Cr(VI) removal by ZCH-2 was 0.106 min−1, which was 8.9 times that of ZCH-0. This indicated that the addition of N-CQDs greatly enhanced the adsorption synergistic photoreduction performance of ZCH-2. After five cycles of adsorption and degradation experiments, the removal efficiency of Cr(VI) still reached 86.6 %. In addition, the leaching amount of zinc in water was only 4.19 mg/L. In the photoreduction process, N-CQDs released electrons to the conduction band of ZnO, and Cr(VI) was reduced to Cr(III) under acidic conditions. N-CQDs formed N-CQDs-metal complexes with Cr(VI), resulting in fluorescence quenching. This work not only provides a feasible reference for the removal of heavy metal pollutants in wastewater treatment, but also opens up a new world for the application of N-CQDs loaded cellulose-based hydrogels in the environmental field.

Qing Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Visualization, Formal analysis. Yong You: Investigation, Software, Visualization. Xingyu Hu: Investigation, Visualization. Danqing Lu: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Qian Wen: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Gang Yu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Wenlei Wang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Tao Xu: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is jointly funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51974115) and the Scientific Research Foundation of Education Office of Hunan Province (No. 22C0111).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2023.128068.

Chem. Eng. J.
